questions.q: The review describes the two-chapter arc as a memory aid. What does each chapter cover?
options: Chapter 23 plans how to build; Chapter 24 defines what to build
Chapter 23 defines what to build; Chapter 24 plans how to build it
Both chapters cover producing and implementing working code
Chapter 23 covers quality assurance; Chapter 24 covers requirements
questions.explanation: The arc is 'define it, then plan how to build it' — Chapter 23 is defining (requirements and feasibility, ideation and modelling, implementation methods) and Chapter 24 is planning (the three approaches, project management, the people side, quality assurance). Option A reverses the order, which is the easy slip; the script stresses 'define what to build in twenty-three, plan how to build it in twenty-four.'

questions.q: Identify the four feasibility dimensions captured by the TECO mnemonic.
options: Technical, Economic, Cost-and-schedule, Operational
Technical, Ethical, Commercial, Organisational
Testing, Economic, Compliance, Operational
Technical, Economic, Communication, Output
questions.explanation: TECO stands for Technical (skills and tech), Economic (benefits versus costs, ROI), Cost-and-schedule (deliver on time and budget with a risk buffer), and Operational (will people actually adopt it). The tempting distractor swaps in 'Ethical' and 'Commercial', but the syllabus dimension is Economic, and ethics is handled separately under PABT.

questions.q: The script contrasts two band levels for writing a requirement. Why is '95th percentile under 300 milliseconds at 200 users' a band-six requirement while 'it should be fast' is band four?
options: It uses more technical vocabulary, which always scores higher
It specifies a non-functional category, and category names earn marks
It is measurable and testable, written with acceptance criteria, so it can be objectively verified
It describes a functional behaviour rather than a quality attribute
questions.explanation: The single most important property of any requirement is that it must be measurable and testable, written with acceptance criteria — 'it should be fast' cannot be verified, but a percentile target at a stated load can. Option B is tempting but wrong: naming a category is not what earns the marks; objective testability is. This is also the through-line that requirements become your later quality criteria and evaluation yardstick.

questions.q: Distinguish the phased, pilot, and parallel implementation methods as the script defines them.
options: Phased splits by user group, pilot splits by feature, parallel runs the old system only
Phased splits by feature, pilot splits by user group, parallel runs both systems at once
Phased and pilot both run both systems at once, while parallel rolls out feature by feature
All three run the old and new systems simultaneously, differing only in cost
questions.explanation: The distinction students lose is exactly this: phased splits by feature (rolled out to everyone), pilot splits by user group (the whole system tried on a small representative group), and parallel runs both systems at once as a safety net. Option A swaps the phased and pilot definitions, which is the classic mix-up the script warns is where marks leak (DiP-PP: Direct, Phased, Parallel, Pilot).

questions.q: A student writes that WAgile 'just combines the good parts of Waterfall and Agile.' According to the review, why is this only a band-four answer?
options: WAgile is not a real approach, so any description of it loses marks
WAgile actually combines only the weak parts of both approaches
It omits the Scope-Time-Cost triangle, which defines WAgile
It fails to explain the when and how — which behaviour applies where governance dominates versus where adaptability dominates
questions.explanation: The exam's key words for WAgile are when and how: apply Waterfall behaviour where governance dominates (compliance, formal reporting, predictable milestones) and Agile behaviour where adaptability dominates (changing requirements, essential user feedback). 'Combines the good parts' with no when-and-how is the band-four answer; done badly WAgile is actually the worst of both, so the 'weak parts' distractor states a real risk but is not the reason this answer scores low.

questions.q: On a Gantt chart, what makes the critical path significant for a project manager?
options: It is the longest chain of dependent tasks, has zero slack, and sets the minimum project duration, so a slip there slips the whole project
It is the chain of tasks with the most slack, so delays there are absorbed
It is the path with the highest cost, which determines the budget
It is the sequence of milestones used for executive reporting
questions.explanation: The critical path is the longest chain of dependent tasks; it sets the minimum project duration and its tasks have zero slack, so any slip on it slips the whole project, whereas a slip within a non-critical task's slack does not move the finish date. The 'most slack' distractor inverts this — slack belongs to non-critical tasks. The script flags 'not checking whether a slipped task is on the critical path' as trap seven.

questions.q: The review insists on a central distinction in the quality-assurance episode. How does quality assurance differ from testing?
options: QA and testing are the same process under two different names
QA detects defects after release; testing prevents them before coding begins
QA applies only to compliance; testing applies only to performance
QA is the process that prevents defects; testing is one activity within it that detects them
questions.explanation: The central distinction is that QA is the overall process that prevents defects — criteria, continual checking, compliance — while testing is one activity within QA that runs test cases to detect defects. The 'QA detects, testing prevents' option reverses the prevent/detect roles, which is the most tempting trap; the script calls conflating QA with testing 'the big one for this chapter' (trap nine).

questions.q: A state health department wants an online appointment-booking and patient-records portal. It must comply with health-privacy law, has a fixed 18-month deadline, executives need formal progress reports, but the patient-facing booking interface must be refined through real user feedback. Which development approach does the review recommend, and why?
options: Pure Waterfall, because the fixed deadline and compliance demand predictable stage-gates throughout
Pure Agile, because the patient interface needs continuous user feedback above all else
WAgile, with the records core handled Waterfall-heavy for compliance and the booking interface Agile-heavy in sprints, under shared stage-gates
Direct cut-over of a single Waterfall build to meet the deadline
questions.explanation: This is the textbook WAgile case: pure Waterfall cannot refine the patient interface through feedback, and pure Agile cannot give regulators and executives formal documentation and predictable milestones, so you run formal stage-gates across the 18 months with the records core Waterfall-heavy and the booking Agile-heavy. Options A and B each capture only one half of the conflicting requirements; WAgile resolves the tension by explaining the when and how, not just 'combines both'.

questions.q: A hospital pharmacy dispensing system is being replaced. Distinguish direct from parallel implementation and recommend one with justification.
options: Direct runs both systems at once; recommend it because duplicate running verifies outputs safely

Direct switches over in a single changeover with no fallback; parallel runs old and new together as a fallback — recommend parallel because the system is safety-critical with zero tolerance for failure
Parallel switches over instantly and cheaply; recommend it to minimise cost on a tight budget
Both methods carry equal risk, so recommend whichever is faster to deploy
questions.explanation: Direct switches entirely to the new system in one changeover — fastest and cheapest but high-risk with no fallback; parallel runs old and new simultaneously, giving a working fallback and verifiable outputs but at the highest cost. For a dispensing system an error could harm or kill a patient, so it is safety-critical with zero tolerance; the higher cost of parallel is justified, which is the cost-versus-safety trade-off the mark depends on. Option A swaps the definitions of direct and parallel.

questions.q: Both Denver airport's baggage system and Healthcare.gov are cited as the same lesson 'in different costumes.' What is that shared lesson?
options: Both failures came from writing buggy code that better programmers would have avoided
Both failures were caused by choosing Agile when Waterfall was required
Both failures happened in defining and planning — before code was ever the problem — so getting Chapters 23–24 wrong dooms the build
Both projects succeeded technically but failed only because of poor marketing
questions.explanation: Denver chose a direct cut-over of a giant untested system with unbounded scope and infeasible plans (a TECO and DiP-PP failure), while Healthcare.gov used Waterfall under a fixed deadline with frozen, flawed requirements and no real load testing — both failures happened in defining and planning, long before code was the problem. Option A misses the through-line: the script stresses the build is doomed no matter how good your code, which is why the module marker is justify, manage, document. Healthcare.gov was a Waterfall failure, so option B is also wrong.
